item.title: 2019 in Review
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
AnôÙothôÙer year went by, and alôÙthough I haven't blogged that much this year, quite a few things did hapôÙpen. Let's take a quick reôÙview of them.

As you know, I atôÙtendôÙed FOSôÙDEM 2019, back in FebruôÙary, and it was an exôÙhilôÙaôÙratôÙing evenôÙt.

I alôÙso took the opôÙporôÙtuôÙniôÙty to dive more inôÙto funcôÙtionôÙal proôÙgramôÙming with Scala, by comôÙpletôÙing the onôÙline course inôÙ ôÙCoursôÙera (which I highôÙly recôÙomôÙmend).

On the open source fronôÙt, I startôÙed with some humôÙble conôÙtriôÙbuôÙtions to the grôÙpc proôÙjecôÙt, with the goal to add asynôÙchroôÙnous supôÙport for the Python imôÙpleôÙmenôÙtaôÙtion.

I alôÙso read many inôÙterôÙestôÙing bookôÙs. Among those about softôÙware enôÙgiôÙneerôÙing, I would alôÙso like to recôÙomôÙmend SoftôÙware CreôÙativôÙiôÙty 2.0 by Robert Glass.

What to look forward to for the 2020? I would like to repeat the FOSDEM experience, and keep on contributing to open source projects, as much as possible. There's also the possibility (however I can't promise anything), that I might start a YouTube channel, with content similar to the one on this blog. But as always there will be more that I can't anticipate; the best is usually the unexpected!

HapôÙpy 2020 to evôÙeryôÙbody!

FOSDEM19 it's just over, and here are my main highlights!

item.title: 2018 in review
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
It was a great year, let's take a look at the main highôÙlightôÙs.

EarôÙly in JanôÙuary, I preôÙsentôÙed a talk at a loôÙcal meeôÙtup, which had a good reôÙcepôÙtion amongst the auôÙdiôÙence. The fist part of the year was a ôÙgreat peôÙriôÙod for learnôÙing more about disôÙtribôÙuted sysôÙtemôÙs, event sourcing, and evenôÙt-ôÙdrivôÙen arôÙchiôÙtecôÙtures. The peak of which was atôÙtendôÙing the KafôÙka sumôÙmit in LonôÙdon.

By late AuôÙgust/earôÙly SepôÙtemôÙber I finôÙished my book Clean code in Python which was a quite chalôÙlengôÙing exôÙpeôÙriôÙence.

It was a great pleaôÙsure to atôÙtend the Python San SeôÙbastiûÀn conôÙferôÙence. I've heard about this conôÙferôÙence in a lightôÙning talk in EuôÙroPyôÙthon, and I was cuôÙriôÙous about it. It was good to meet a group of friendôÙly new peoôÙple, and havôÙing the opôÙporôÙtuôÙniôÙty to preôÙsenôÙt there (an enôÙhanced verôÙsion of the talk preôÙsentôÙed at the meetôÙup in JanôÙuary). BeôÙsides, the city is amazôÙing, so it's cerôÙtainôÙly someôÙthing to reôÙpeat!

BeôÙsides some imôÙproveôÙments in my conôÙfigôÙuôÙraôÙtion files (vim and dot files), and to ôÙsome of my othôÙer pubôÙlic reposôÙiôÙtoôÙries, there wasôÙn't that much room for open ôÙsource conôÙtriôÙbuôÙtionôÙs. On the othôÙer hand, there was a good deal of learnôÙing: I've read some fanôÙtasôÙtic books about softôÙware enôÙgiôÙneerôÙing (such as Facts and ôÙfalôÙlaôÙcies of softôÙware enôÙgiôÙneerôÙing, ObôÙjecôÙt-oriôÙentôÙed softôÙware conôÙstrucôÙtion, and more), which unôÙforôÙtuôÙnateôÙly I wasôÙn't able to covôÙer in blog posts (but mayôÙbe in the fuôÙture I will), and I sucôÙcessôÙfulôÙly comôÙpletôÙed the maôÙchine learnôÙing ôÙcourse ofôÙfered by CoursôÙera, which was amazôÙing. AôÙgain, maybe in a fuôÙture post I will be able to propôÙerôÙly reôÙview it, but for now I can just tell you that is highôÙly recôÙomôÙmendôÙable, if you're lookôÙing forôÙward to learn a ton and at the same time have fun solvôÙing proôÙgramôÙming asôÙsignôÙments.

All in alôÙl, it was a year with a lot of posôÙiôÙtive notes. LookôÙing forôÙward to an aôÙmazôÙing 2019 for evôÙeryôÙone!

item.title: Subtleties of Python
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
In our proôÙfesôÙsion, atôÙtenôÙtion to deôÙtail is of utôÙmost imôÙporôÙtance. Any good ôÙsoftôÙware enôÙgiôÙneer unôÙderôÙstands that deôÙtails matôÙter a lot, as they make the dôÙifôÙferôÙence beôÙtween a workôÙing unit or a disôÙasôÙter 1.

This is why clean code is not just about forôÙmatôÙting or arôÙrangôÙing the code. It's neiôÙther a foible. It is inôÙstead, payôÙing atôÙtenôÙtion exôÙactôÙly to those deôÙtails that will make a big difôÙferôÙence in proôÙducôÙtion.

Let's see some exôÙamôÙples of this in Python.

Read moreãÎ (4 min remaining to read)

item.title: Notes on the Kafka Summit London 2018
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
These are some notes and takeôÙaways on the reôÙcentôÙly celôÙeôÙbratôÙed KafôÙka SumôÙmit 2018 in LonôÙdon.

The conôÙferôÙence was orôÙgaôÙnized in three parôÙalôÙlel tracks for sesôÙsions that were ôÙcovôÙerôÙing stream proôÙcessôÙing, pipeliôÙnes, and inôÙterôÙnalôÙs. To get a good exôÙpeôÙriôÙence, I atôÙtendôÙed talks of the three typeôÙs, but with a litôÙtle prefôÙerôÙence toôÙwardôÙs inôÙterôÙnals and streamôÙs.

It was a two-ôÙday conôÙferôÙence with lots of valuôÙable techôÙniôÙcal conôÙtent, aweôÙsome talkôÙs, speakôÙerôÙs, and a lot more. Here are the highôÙlightôÙs.

Read moreãÎ (1 min remaining to read)

item.title: Exploring Generators and Coroutines
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
Let's reôÙvisôÙit the idea of genôÙerôÙaôÙtors in Python, in orôÙder to unôÙderôÙstand how the supôÙport for corouôÙtines was achieved in latôÙest verôÙsions of Python (3.6, at the time of this writôÙing).

By reôÙviewôÙing the mileôÙstones on genôÙerôÙaôÙtors, chronoôÙlogôÙiôÙcalôÙly, we can get a betôÙter idea of the evoôÙluôÙtion that lead to asynôÙchroôÙnous proôÙgramôÙming in Python.

We will reôÙview the main changes in Python that reôÙlate to genôÙerôÙaôÙtors and asynôÙchronous proôÙgramôÙming, startôÙing with PEP-255 (SimôÙple GenôÙerôÙaôÙtors), PEP-342 (ôÙCorouôÙtines via EnôÙhanced GenôÙerôÙaôÙtors), PEP-380 (SynôÙtax for delôÙeôÙgatôÙing to a ôÙSubôÙ-ôÙGenôÙerôÙaôÙtor), and finôÙishôÙing with PEP-525 (AsynôÙchronous GenôÙerôÙaôÙtors).

Read moreãÎ (16 min remaining to read)

item.title: 2017 in Review
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
A reôÙcap of the main events and posts throughôÙout the year that is endôÙing.

This year I atôÙtendôÙed some conôÙferôÙences overôÙseas, where I preôÙsentôÙed talkôÙs. First, in June, I went to the beauôÙtiôÙful city of Prague for PyôÙCon CZ 2017. At first, I ôÙgot onôÙly one talk apôÙproved, DisôÙcovôÙerôÙing DeôÙscripôÙtors, which was a new ôÙtopic I was preôÙsentôÙing. A few days beôÙfore the conôÙferôÙence startôÙed, anôÙothôÙer sôÙpeakôÙer had to step down, so I was asked to fill in the slot, to which I agreed ôÙby proposôÙing my talk of clean code in Python. That meant I preôÙsentôÙed one the ôÙfirst two days of the conôÙferôÙence, and then atôÙtendôÙed the workôÙshop on the last ôÙday (SatôÙurôÙday).

The conôÙferôÙence was nice, and as alôÙways, a great opôÙporôÙtuôÙniôÙty for netôÙworkôÙing and learnôÙing.

JuôÙly was the month for EuôÙroPyôÙthon, a conôÙferôÙence that is alôÙways aweôÙsome. This year, I did not have a talk schedôÙuled beôÙforeôÙhand, as I did last year, but ôÙsurôÙprisôÙingôÙly enough, on the lightôÙning talk sesôÙsions of MonôÙday they anôÙnounced a free slot for the TuesôÙday, to which I volôÙunôÙteer by subôÙmitôÙting my new talk, "DisôÙcovôÙerôÙing DeôÙscripôÙtors". So, once again, anôÙothôÙer unôÙexôÙpectôÙed (but glad), opôÙporôÙtuôÙniôÙty that I had to present a talk at a good conôÙferôÙence.

On othôÙer topôÙicôÙs, I atôÙtendôÙed sevôÙerôÙal meeôÙtups of Python and Go, and beôÙcame more inôÙvolved in disôÙtribôÙuted sysôÙtemôÙs.

It was alôÙso a good year to work more with DockôÙer, and startôÙed to learn more about KuôÙberôÙnetes.

ReôÙgardôÙing conôÙtriôÙbuôÙtions to open source, I reôÙleased new verôÙsions of most of the proôÙjects I have on GitHub, but there is more. I sent the first sucôÙcessôÙful ôÙpatch to CPython in GitHub (imôÙporôÙtant note: at the beôÙginôÙning of this year, the CPython project moved to GitHub). It was a simôÙple change on the docôÙuôÙmenôÙtaôÙtion, about deôÙscripôÙtors, that got quickôÙly merged, but a good head start.

AnôÙothôÙer nice conôÙtriôÙbuôÙtion is that, durôÙing the sprints at EuôÙroPyôÙthon, I worked aôÙlong the pypy team, and sent some comôÙmits fixôÙing changes for Python 3.6. It was an amazôÙing exôÙpeôÙriôÙence to learn more about the proôÙjecôÙt, and about CPython itôÙself. CerôÙtainôÙly someôÙthing I look forôÙward to conôÙtinôÙue doôÙing.

For the new year, I exôÙpect more conôÙtriôÙbuôÙtions to open source, mainôÙly on the same projects (CPython and pypy), and reôÙlease more open source liôÙbraries. AlôÙso more conôÙferôÙences, and as alôÙways, room for the best which is usuôÙalôÙly the unôÙexôÙpectôÙed.

item.title: Refuse the temptation to pass
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
This is an opinôÙion I someôÙtimes reôÙmemôÙber when seeôÙing some code. Don't take it as a strong adôÙvice, or a rule, but inôÙstead as a genôÙerôÙal guideôÙline that might help yôÙou to imôÙprove the code slightôÙly.

On this post I will share a tiny and opinôÙionôÙatôÙed arôÙguôÙment about why there are usuôÙalôÙly betôÙter things to do in the code inôÙstead of just pass.

Read moreãÎ (1 min remaining to read)
